rationale: TIEBREAKER: O1 is right; G3's finding is a taxonomy artifact, not a decomposition defect. AC1/AC6/AC7 are COMPOUND ACs the SPEC ITSELF authored to span the substrate/consumer edge: AC1 names TestScanOrphanedClosedBeads_ErrorPreserving (lifecycle) beside two TestApproveImpl_* proofs; AC6 names TestPendingObligationPredicate (complete); AC7 the spec EXPLICITLY splits into two named tests citing the import cycle. Each NAMED proof-test has exactly ONE bead home (the bead owning its package) — no test is authored twice; nothing is double-owned. What spans two beads is the AC-to-test mapping, inherent to a spec-written compound AC. G3's literal fix is INFEASIBLE: I verified approve does not import complete today; Bead 2 adds approve->complete, so a white-box package-complete test calling ApproveImpl cycles (complete->approve->complete). TestCompleteRun_RegatesAlreadyClosedOrphan drives complete.Run (a complete concern) and physically cannot move to Bead 2's approve-only scope without either the cycle or dragging internal/complete test files into an internal/approve bead. The substrate proofs cannot move. Independently: AC1-AC13 all homed, no phantom proofs, R1 four legs + R2 + R3 + Option-B homed, chain=2<=3, 3 work_chunks. The table's '(x half lands in Bead 1)' annotations already state this honestly.

findings.area: AC provenance parity (G3 vs O1 adjudication)
findings.detail: G3's REQUEST_CHANGES is OVERRULED as a taxonomy artifact. AC1/AC6/AC7 are compound acceptance criteria the approved spec itself wrote to span two packages (spec AC1 names the lifecycle test; AC6 names the complete predicate test; AC7's own text mandates a two-test split with the explicit import-cycle reason). Each individual proof test maps to exactly one bead (its package owner); none is authored in two beads. The plan designates Bead 2 as the AC completion/verification owner (the gate is the headline behavior, verifiable only once Bead 2 lands) and lands Bead 1's tests as prerequisite substrate — which is precisely O1's honest reading. No real double-ownership exists.

Verified at 57d043fb: internal/approve imports {adr,bead,executor,frontmatter,guard,phase,recording,state,validate,workspace} and NOT internal/complete (only a comment at complete.go:486 references approve, and complete does not import approve). Bead 2 adds approve->complete for CheckPendingObligations/PanelGateRoots. A white-box test in package complete calling ApproveImpl would compile internal/approve into complete's test binary, forming complete->approve->complete — a genuine Go build cycle. TestCompleteRun_RegatesAlreadyClosedOrphan tests complete.Run's re-gate/converge flow (a complete-package concern, driving complete's seams), so it cannot be an approve-side ApproveImpl test nor move into Bead 2's internal/approve-only scope. G3's fix ('make Bead 2 run every proof') is therefore physically infeasible for AC7(a); the substrate test cannot relocate.

findings.area: No AC dropped / no phantom proof
findings.detail: Provenance table rows exist for AC1-AC13; every named proof matches a test the spec's Acceptance Criteria / Validation Proofs section names (TestApproveImpl_OrphanRefuses, _OrphanInfraErrorFailsClosed, _OrphanExemptions, _DeletedBranchNoRefusal, _HatchDoesNotBypassOrphan, _AdvisorySlotNaming, _ObligationBackstop, _CorruptPlanRefuses, _PassesAfterOrphanSettled, _UnreadableRefStoreAbortsPreScan, _WorktreeEnumRefusesDespiteProbeMiss; TestScanOrphanedClosedBeads_ErrorPreserving; TestPendingObligationPredicate; TestCompleteRun_RegatesAlreadyClosedOrphan; TestFinalizeEpic_MergesOnlyWorktreeRealBranches; TestLifecycleOwnershipExactlyOneWorkflowClaimant; extended TestApproveImplCallOrder anchor). No phantom, none unnamed by the spec.

R1's legs all homed: orphan error-preserving scan (Bead2 leg1/AC1), worktree-enum Option-B leg (Bead2 leg2/AC13), probe fail-open never-false-refuse (AC2d), structural Facts 1&2 (AC11 Bead2 / AC12 Bead1). R2 advisory (Bead2 step5/AC5). R3 obligation backstop (Bead2 leg3/AC6 with predicate substrate in Bead1). Longest chain 1->2 = 2, within <=3. Frontmatter work_chunks = 3 (deps: 1=[], 2=[1], 3=[]); G3 confirmed `mindspec validate plan` exit 0 and 1:1 chunk-to-section alignment. Package-per-bead decomposition is the correct minimal split; the alternative (substrate tests in Bead 2) is strictly worse.

The Provenance table's inline annotations ('lifecycle half lands in Bead 1', 'predicate half lands in Bead 1', '(a) lands in Bead 1') already disclose the substrate/consumer split honestly and are the correct description. A one-line legend stating 'compound ACs spanning the substrate/consumer edge name the completion-owner (later) bead in the Bead column and annotate the prerequisite half inline' would marginally aid a reader but is pure polish, NOT required to approve. I do not condition my APPROVE on it.
